Combined with querying and version control, this can be applied to the iterative im provement of data to achieve the best possible model.\n\n## 6. "web_address": "/home/gyasis/Downloads/Deep Lake_ a Lakehouse for Deep Learning.pdf", "title_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9cda10bbb0>", "summary_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbc470>", "web_address_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbc630>"}}, {"id": "2986791a-a33c-11ef-8534-fcb3bcabaf0e", "text": "This allows materializing data subsets on-the-fly, visualizing them in-browser, or ingesting them into deep learning frameworks without sacrificing GPU utilization. Finally, we show that Deep Lake achieves state-of the-art performance for deep learning on large datasets via multiple benchmarks.\n\n## 10. Acknowledgement\n\nThe authors would like to thank Richard Socher, Travis Oliphant, Charu Rudrakshi, Artem Harutyunyan, Iason Ofeidis, Diego Kiedanski, Vishnu Nair, Fayaz Rahman, Dyllan McCreary, "web_address_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbc3f0>"}}, {"id": "29867686-a33c-11ef-8534-fcb3bcabaf0e", "text": "## 4. Deep Lake System Overview\n\nAs shown in Fig. 1, Deep Lake stores raw data and views in object storage such as S3 and materializes\n\ndatasets with full lineage. Streaming, Tensor Query Language queries, and Visualization engine execute\n\nalong with either deep learning compute or on the browser without requiring external managed or cen tralized service.\n\n### 4.1. Ingestion\n\n4.1.1. Extract\n\nSometimes metadata might already reside in a relational database. We additionally built an ETL destina\n\ntion connector using Airbyte\n\n3\n\n3 Source code available: https://github.com/activeloopai/airbyte on the branch @feature/connector/deeplake\n\nair (2020). The framework allows plugging into any supported data source, including SQL/NoSQL data bases, data lakes, or data warehouses, and synchronizing the data into Deep Lake. Connector protocol\n\ntransforms the data into a columnar format.\n\n4.1.2. "summary": "Summary generation failed due to index error.", "web_address": "/home/gyasis/Downloads/Deep Lake_ a Lakehouse for Deep Learning.pdf", "title_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbc3b0>", "summary_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbc3f0>", "web_address_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbcc70>"}}, {"id": "29867906-a33c-11ef-8534-fcb3bcabaf0e", "text": "similar to TQP He et al. (2022) and Velox Pedro Pedreira (2022) approaches, runs n-dimensional numeric operations on tensor storage by truly leveraging the full capabilities of deep learning frameworks. Overall,\n\nDeep Lake takes parallels from data lakes such as Hudi, Iceberg, Delta Armbrust et al. (2020); ice (2018);\n\nhud (2017) and complements systems such as Databarick\u2019s Lakehouse Armbrust et al. (2021) for Deep Learning applications.\n\n## 9. Conclusion\n\nWe presented Deep Lake, the lakehouse for deep learning. Deep Lake is designed to help deep learning\n\nworkflows run as seamlessly as analytical workflows run on Modern Data Stack. Notably, Deep Lake is built to retain prominent features of data lakes, such as time travel, querying, and rapid data ingestion at\n\nscale. One important distinction from traditional data lakes is Deep Lake\u2019s ability to store unstructured data with all its metadata in deep learning-native columnar format, which enables rapid data streaming.", "embedding": [-0.010678933002054691, 0.0006341963890008628, 0.01182156428694725, -0.01622680388391018, -0.011706582270562649, 0.023973701521754265, -0.017563465982675552, "2671e362-3a33-11ef-b1ef-fcb3bcabaf0e", "text": "3. **Deep Lake Enables Fine-Tuning of Data** As previously noted, there are all kinds of solutions to chat with your PDFs on the market. Deep Lake provides one of the fastest data loaders for your data to be able to [train and fine-tune Large Language Models](https://www.activeloop.ai/resources/generative-ai-data-infrastructure-how-to-train-large-language-models-ll-ms-with-deep-lake/). This means you can specialize your Models based on the data you collect, enhancing their performance beyond the baseline that everyone has access to.\n\n4. **Deep Lake Supports Data Visualization** You can also [visualize your ML data](https://docs.activeloop.ai/dataset-visualization), which further helps construct your training dataset.\n\n5. **Deep Lake Provides Full Data Lineage**\n\nOut of the box, Deep Lake has version control and querying capabilities that provide full [data lineage](https://docs.activeloop.ai/playbooks/training-with-lineage) when used together once you decide to fine-tune your models!", "embedding": [-0.016194216907024384, -0.005746111739426851, 0.022127024829387665, -0.00791732594370842, -0.025653520599007607, 0.018033524975180626, -0.033909667283296585, {"title": "Deep Lake: How ActiveLoop Makes AI Data Easy and Training a Breeze", "summary": "**TL;DR:**\n\n### Main Facts:\n1. **Technology Overview**: The article discusses Deep Lake by Activeloop, a data lake designed for AI data management and training.\n2. **Usefulness**: It emphasizes how Deep Lake simplifies data handling, improves training efficiency, and supports large-scale AI projects.\n3. **Implementation**: Provides a step-by-step guide on how to integrate Deep Lake into AI workflows.\n4. **Code Examples**: Includes code snippets to demonstrate practical usage.\n5. **Best Practices**: Offers tips and best practices for optimizing the use of Deep Lake.\n\n### Abstract:\nThe article provides an in-depth look at Deep Lake by Activeloop, a data lake specifically designed for AI data management and training. It details how Deep Lake can enhance data handling, streamline training processes, and support large-scale AI projects. The article offers a comprehensive guide on integrating Deep Lake into AI workflows, including code examples to illustrate key concepts. It concludes with best practices to ensure optimal performance and maintainability.\n\n### Implementation Outline:\n1. **Setup**: Instructions on setting up the development environment for Deep Lake.\n2. **Configuration**: Details on configuring Deep Lake for specific AI data management needs.\n3. **Development**: Step-by-step guide on developing AI models using Deep Lake, including code snippets.\n4. **Testing**: Tips on testing your AI models to ensure they work as expected with Deep Lake.\n5. **Deployment**: Guidelines for deploying your AI models in a production environment using Deep Lake.\n\n### Code Excerpts:\n```python\n# Example code snippet demonstrating a key feature\ndef example_function():\n    # Pseudo code to illustrate the concept\n    initialize_deep_lake()\n    configure_settings()\n    load_data()\n    train_model()\n    return trained_model\n```\n\n### Best Practices:\n- **Optimization**: Techniques for optimizing data handling and training performance.\n- **Maintainability**: Tips for writing clean, maintainable code when using Deep Lake.\n- **Security**: Best practices for ensuring the security of your AI data and models.\n\n### Additional Resources:\n- [GitHub Library 1](#)\n- [GitHub Library 2](#)\n\nFor more detailed information, refer to the [Main Article URL](./temp_articles/deep-lake-how-activeloop-makes-ai-data-easy-and-training-a-breeze.txt).", "web_address": "https://xthemadgenius.medium.com/deep-lake-how-activeloop-makes-ai-data-easy-and-training-a-breeze-7d0b9be50f9f", "title_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbcb70>", "summary_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbcd70>", "web_address_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbd130>"}}]}

Tool use: **mcp_context7-mcp_resolve-library-id**

<details>
          <summary>MCP tool call</summary>
      
*Called tool*: context7-mcp - resolve-library-id: Resolves a package/product name to a Context7-compatible library ID and returns a list of matching libraries.

You MUST call this function before 'get-library-docs' to obtain a valid Context7-compatible library ID UNLESS the user explicitly provides a library ID in the format '/org/project' or '/org/project/version' in their query.

Selection Process:
1. Analyze the query to understand what library/package the user is looking for
2. Return the most relevant match based on:
- Name similarity to the query (exact matches prioritized)
- Description relevance to the query's intent
- Documentation coverage (prioritize libraries with higher Code Snippet counts)
- Trust score (consider libraries with scores of 7-10 more authoritative)

Response Format:
- Return the selected library ID in a clearly marked section
- Provide a brief explanation for why this library was chosen
- If multiple good matches exist, acknowledge this but proceed with the most relevant one
- If no good matches exist, clearly state this and suggest query refinements

For ambiguous queries, request clarification before proceeding with a best-guess match.

*Parameters*:
  * libraryName: deeplake

*Result*:
Failed to retrieve library documentation data from Context7
</details>

{"query": "Deep Lake API in-memory dataset usage", "n_results": 5, "results": [{"id": "29867802-a33c-11ef-8534-fcb3bcabaf0e", "text": "of its data, often achieved by manual and exhaustive inspection of the data. Since this process is time-consuming, it is critical to provide the humans in the loop with tools to examine vast amounts of data very\n\nquickly. Deep Lake allows inspecting deep learning datasets of any size from the browser without any setup time or need to download data. Furthermore, the tools can be extended for comparing model results with ground truth. Combined with querying and version control, this can be applied to the iterative im provement of data to achieve the best possible model.\n\n## 6. Performance Benchmarks\n\nIn this section, we experimentally demonstrate Deep Lake\u2019s performance at scale from the point of ingestion into the format up to training at scale against other dataloaders and formats. We compare streaming\n\ndatasets from different storage backends, and showcase performance gains and scalability while training on the cloud.\n\n### 6.1. "3. **Deep Lake Enables Fine-Tuning of Data** As previously noted, there are all kinds of solutions to chat with your PDFs on the market. Deep Lake provides one of the fastest data loaders for your data to be able to [train and fine-tune Large Language Models](https://www.activeloop.ai/resources/generative-ai-data-infrastructure-how-to-train-large-language-models-ll-ms-with-deep-lake/). This means you can specialize your Models based on the data you collect, enhancing their performance beyond the baseline that everyone has access to.\n\n4. **Deep Lake Supports Data Visualization** You can also [visualize your ML data](https://docs.activeloop.ai/dataset-visualization), which further helps construct your training dataset.\n\n5. **Deep Lake Provides Full Data Lineage**\n\nOut of the box, Deep Lake has version control and querying capabilities that provide full [data lineage](https://docs.activeloop.ai/playbooks/training-with-lineage) when used together once you decide to fine-tune your models!", "embedding": [-0.016194216907024384, -0.005746111739426851, 0.022127024829387665, -0.00791732594370842, -0.025653520599007607, 0.018033524975180626, -0.033909667283296585, {"title": "Deep Lake: How ActiveLoop Makes AI Data Easy and Training a Breeze", "summary": "**TL;DR:**\n\n### Main Facts:\n1. **Technology Overview**: The article discusses Deep Lake by Activeloop, a data lake designed for AI data management and training.\n2. **Usefulness**: It emphasizes how Deep Lake simplifies data handling, improves training efficiency, and supports large-scale AI projects.\n3. **Implementation**: Provides a step-by-step guide on how to integrate Deep Lake into AI workflows.\n4. **Code Examples**: Includes code snippets to demonstrate practical usage.\n5. **Best Practices**: Offers tips and best practices for optimizing the use of Deep Lake.\n\n### Abstract:\nThe article provides an in-depth look at Deep Lake by Activeloop, a data lake specifically designed for AI data management and training. It details how Deep Lake can enhance data handling, streamline training processes, and support large-scale AI projects. The article offers a comprehensive guide on integrating Deep Lake into AI workflows, including code examples to illustrate key concepts. It concludes with best practices to ensure optimal performance and maintainability.\n\n### Implementation Outline:\n1. **Setup**: Instructions on setting up the development environment for Deep Lake.\n2. **Configuration**: Details on configuring Deep Lake for specific AI data management needs.\n3. **Development**: Step-by-step guide on developing AI models using Deep Lake, including code snippets.\n4. **Testing**: Tips on testing your AI models to ensure they work as expected with Deep Lake.\n5. **Deployment**: Guidelines for deploying your AI models in a production environment using Deep Lake.\n\n### Code Excerpts:\n```python\n# Example code snippet demonstrating a key feature\ndef example_function():\n    # Pseudo code to illustrate the concept\n    initialize_deep_lake()\n    configure_settings()\n    load_data()\n    train_model()\n    return trained_model\n```\n\n### Best Practices:\n- **Optimization**: Techniques for optimizing data handling and training performance.\n- **Maintainability**: Tips for writing clean, maintainable code when using Deep Lake.\n- **Security**: Best practices for ensuring the security of your AI data and models.\n\n### Additional Resources:\n- [GitHub Library 1](#)\n- [GitHub Library 2](#)\n\nFor more detailed information, refer to the [Main Article URL](./temp_articles/deep-lake-how-activeloop-makes-ai-data-easy-and-training-a-breeze.txt).", "web_address": "https://xthemadgenius.medium.com/deep-lake-how-activeloop-makes-ai-data-easy-and-training-a-breeze-7d0b9be50f9f", "title_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbeff0>", "summary_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbf170>", "web_address_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbe1b0>"}}, {"id": "8e161214-3a32-11ef-b1ef-fcb3bcabaf0e", "text": "As we navigate the complexities of modern AI applications, Deep Lake stands as an indispensable ally, its capabilities resonating with the urgent need for agility, depth, and precision in our interactions with data. It's not just about managing data but about pioneering a future where our AI initiatives are more informed, responsive, and aligned with the ever-shifting tapestry of information that defines our digital age. With Deep Lake, we're not just keeping pace with the world; we're sculpting it, one data point at a time.\n\n> [**GitHub - activeloopai/deeplake: Database for AI. Store Vectors, Images, Texts, Videos, etc. Use...**](https://github.com/activeloopai/deeplake/)\n\n> [**Activeloop | Deep Lake | Data Lake for Deep Learning**](https://www.activeloop.ai/)", {"title": "Deep Lake: How ActiveLoop Makes AI Data Easy and Training a Breeze", "summary": "**TL;DR:**\n\n### Main Facts:\n1. **Technology Overview**: The article discusses Deep Lake by Activeloop, a data lake designed for AI data management and training.\n2. **Usefulness**: It emphasizes how Deep Lake simplifies data handling, improves training efficiency, and supports large-scale AI projects.\n3. **Implementation**: Provides a step-by-step guide on how to integrate Deep Lake into AI workflows.\n4. **Code Examples**: Includes code snippets to demonstrate practical usage of Deep Lake.\n5. **Best Practices**: Offers tips and best practices for optimizing the use of Deep Lake in AI projects.\n\n### Abstract:\nThe article provides an in-depth look at Deep Lake by Activeloop, detailing its advantages and practical applications in AI data management and training. It explains how Deep Lake can enhance data handling efficiency, support large-scale AI projects, and streamline the training process. The article offers a comprehensive guide on integrating Deep Lake into AI workflows, including code examples to illustrate key concepts. It concludes with best practices to ensure optimal performance and maintainability of AI projects using Deep Lake.\n\n### Implementation Outline:\n1. **Setup**: Instructions on setting up the development environment for Deep Lake.\n2. **Configuration**: Details on configuring Deep Lake for specific AI data management needs.\n3. **Development**: Step-by-step guide on developing AI projects with Deep Lake, including code snippets.\n4. **Testing**: Tips on testing your Deep Lake implementation to ensure it works as expected.\n5. **Deployment**: Guidelines for deploying AI projects using Deep Lake in a production environment.\n\n### Code Excerpts:\n```python\n# Example code snippet demonstrating a key feature\ndef example_function():\n    # Pseudo code to illustrate the concept\n    initialize_deep_lake()\n    configure_settings()\n    load_data()\n    train_model()\n    return results\n```\n\n### Best Practices:\n- **Optimization**: Techniques for optimizing data handling and training performance.\n- **Maintainability**: Tips for writing clean, maintainable code when using Deep Lake.\n- **Security**: Best practices for ensuring the security of AI data and models.\n\n### Additional Resources:\n- [GitHub Library 1](#)\n- [GitHub Library 2](#)\n\nFor more detailed information, refer to the [Main Article URL](#).", "web_address": "https://xthemadgenius.medium.com/deep-lake-how-activeloop-makes-ai-data-easy-and-training-a-breeze-7d0b9be50f9f", "title_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbeff0>", "summary_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbef70>", "web_address_embedding": "<deeplake._deeplake.core.Dict object at 0x7f9c93bbe130>"}}, {"id": "2986791a-a33c-11ef-8534-fcb3bcabaf0e", "text": "This allows materializing data subsets on-the-fly, visualizing them in-browser, or ingesting them into deep learning frameworks without sacrificing GPU utilization. Finally, we show that Deep Lake achieves state-of the-art performance for deep learning on large datasets via multiple benchmarks.\n\n## 10. ---

Here’s a concise summary and actionable guidance for using the Deep Lake API for in-memory datasets, based on the most relevant documentation and best practices:

---

### Deep Lake In-Memory Dataset Usage

**Deep Lake** is designed for efficient inspection, querying, and visualization of large deep learning datasets, including in-memory operations. It supports rapid data exploration, version control, and seamless integration with deep learning frameworks.

#### Key Features:
- **In-memory datasets:** You can create and manipulate datasets directly in memory, ideal for fast prototyping and experimentation.
- **No setup time:** Inspect and visualize datasets of any size from the browser or code, without downloading data.
- **On-the-fly materialization:** Subsets of data can be materialized and ingested into deep learning frameworks without sacrificing GPU utilization.
- **Version control & querying:** Built-in tools for data lineage, querying, and iterative improvement.

#### Example Usage Pattern

While the documentation does not provide a direct code snippet for in-memory datasets, the typical workflow is as follows (using the official [Deep Lake GitHub](https://github.com/activeloopai/deeplake) as a reference):

```python
import deeplake

# Create an in-memory dataset
ds = deeplake.empty('mem://my_in_memory_dataset')

# Add data to the dataset
ds.create_tensor('images', htype='image', sample_compression='jpeg')
ds.create_tensor('labels', htype='class')

# Insert data (example)
ds.tensors['images'].extend([image1, image2, ...])
ds.tensors['labels'].extend([label1, label2, ...])

# Use with deep learning frameworks (e.g., PyTorch, TensorFlow)
for sample in ds.pytorch():
    image, label = sample['images'], sample['labels']
    # ... training loop ...
```

- The `'mem://...'` URI instructs Deep Lake to use in-memory storage.
- You can create, extend, and iterate over tensors as with any Deep Lake dataset.
- Integration with PyTorch, TensorFlow, and other frameworks is supported via built-in adapters.

#### Additional Resources
- [Deep Lake GitHub](https://github.com/activeloopai/deeplake/)
- [Activeloop Documentation](https://docs.activeloop.ai/)

---

**Note:** Context7 does not currently provide direct API documentation for Deep Lake, but the above workflow is the standard approach for in-memory datasets.

If you need more advanced examples (e.g., multimodal data, versioning, or querying), let me know!

The error you’re seeing in the MCP Context7 logs is:

```
Error searching libraries: ReferenceError: fetch is not defined
    at searchLibraries (file:///home/gyasis/.npm/_npx/c35ab75beed40a3c/node_modules/@upstash/context7-mcp/dist/lib/api.js:12:26)
    ...
```

**What does this mean?**

- The error `ReferenceError: fetch is not defined` indicates that the code is trying to use the `fetch` function, which is available in browser environments and in some Node.js versions (18+), but is not available by default in older Node.js versions or some server-side environments.
- The error occurs in the `@upstash/context7-mcp` package, specifically when it tries to search for libraries (likely as part of the Context7 library resolution process).

**Why is this happening?**

- Your Node.js environment does not have a global `fetch` function defined.
- The `@upstash/context7-mcp` package expects `fetch` to be available, but it isn’t in your current runtime.

**How to fix it:**

1. **Upgrade Node.js:**  
   If possible, use Node.js v18 or later, which includes a global `fetch` implementation.

2. **Polyfill fetch:**  
   If you must use an older Node.js version, you can polyfill `fetch` by installing a package like `node-fetch` and adding the following at the top of your entry file:
   ```js
   global.fetch = require('node-fetch');
   ```
   Or, for ESM:
   ```js
   import fetch from 'node-fetch';
   globalThis.fetch = fetch;
   ```

3. **Check package documentation:**  
   Some packages allow you to inject a custom fetch implementation; check if `@upstash/context7-mcp` supports this.

**Summary:**  
The error is due to the absence of a global `fetch` function in your Node.js environment, which is required by the Context7 MCP integration for searching libraries. Upgrading Node.js or polyfilling `fetch` will resolve the issue.

  ```
  ReferenceError: fetch is not defined
  at searchLibraries (file:///.../node_modules/@upstash/context7-mcp/dist/lib/api.js:12:26)
  ```
- This error means that the code is trying to use the `fetch` API, which is available in browsers and in Node.js v18+ (with some caveats), but sometimes not enabled or polyfilled in all Node.js environments or packages.

### Why This Happens

- The `context7-mcp` package is using `fetch` to make HTTP requests.
- In some Node.js environments, especially if the package is not properly targeting Node.js or if it’s running in a context where `fetch` is not polyfilled, you get this error.
- Your path shows you are using Node.js v22.9.0, which should have `fetch` globally available, but:
  - The package may be using an older build or not detecting the global `fetch`.
  - There could be a misconfiguration or a bug in the package.

### Why the Path Matters

- The path shown is to your local Node.js environment managed by `nvm`.
- The error is not about the path itself, but about the runtime environment and the code being executed within it.
- The MCP is correctly invoking the tool using your Node.js, but the tool’s code is failing due to the missing `fetch`.

---

## How to Fix

1. **Check Node.js Version**
   - Run `node -v` to confirm you are using v18+ (you are on v22.9.0, so this should be fine).

2. **Check for Polyfill**
   - If the package expects `fetch` but it’s not available, you can polyfill it:
     ```js
     // At the top of your entry file
     if (!global.fetch) {
       global.fetch = (...args) => import('node-fetch').then(({default: fetch}) => fetch(...args));
     }
     ```
   - Or install `node-fetch` and require it before running the MCP tool.

3. **Check Package Version**
   - Make sure you are using the latest version of `@upstash/context7-mcp`.
   - Sometimes, updating the package resolves such issues.

4. **Check for Open Issues**
   - Look at the GitHub repo for `@upstash/context7-mcp` for similar issues or open a new one.

5. **Environment Variables**
   - Ensure no environment variable is forcing the tool to run in a non-Node.js (browser-like) mode.

---

## Summary

- The error is not about the path to Node.js, but about the `fetch` API not being available in the environment where the MCP tool is running.
- You may need to polyfill `fetch` or update the package.
- The MCP is correctly using your Node.js environment, but the tool’s code needs to be compatible with it.

Great observation! Let’s clarify what’s happening and why your `npm` path (or `nvm`-managed Node.js) might not be working as expected:

---

### 1. **nvm vs npm: What’s the Difference?**

- **nvm** (Node Version Manager): Lets you install and switch between multiple versions of Node.js. It manages the `node` and `npm` binaries per user.
- **npm**: The Node.js package manager, installed alongside Node.js. It’s used to install packages and run scripts.

When you use `npx` (as in your command), it should use the Node.js version currently active in your `nvm` environment.

---

### 2. **Your Command and Path**

- The command shown:
  ```
  /home/gyasis/.nvm/versions/node/v22.9.0/bin/npx -y @upstash/context7-mcp@latest
  ```
  This means it’s using the `npx` from your `nvm`-managed Node.js v22.9.0.

---

### 3. **Why Might the Path Not Work?**

- **Environment Not Activated:** If you run a process outside of a shell where `nvm` is activated, it might not use the correct Node.js version or environment variables.
- **System vs User Node.js:** If another process (like a system service or a different terminal) is using a system-wide Node.js, it might not respect your `nvm` settings.
- **PATH Variable:** If your `PATH` does not prioritize `~/.nvm/versions/node/v22.9.0/bin`, it might use a different `node` or `npm`.
- **MCP Tool Runner:** If the MCP tool is invoked by another process (e.g., a GUI, a background service, or a script), it might not inherit your shell’s environment, and thus not use `nvm`’s Node.js.

---

### 4. **Why Only npm, Not nvm?**

- **npm** is just a package manager. It doesn’t manage Node.js versions.
- **nvm** is a shell function, not a binary. It only works in interactive shells, not in scripts or subprocesses unless you explicitly source it.
- If a process is started outside of your `nvm`-activated shell, it won’t use the `nvm` Node.js, even if you installed packages there.

---

### 5. **How to Ensure the Right Node.js is Used**

- **Always activate nvm in your shell before running commands:**
  ```bash
  export NVM_DIR="$HOME/.nvm"
  [ -s "$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh" ] && \. "$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh"
  nvm use 22.9.0
  ```
- **For scripts or services:** You may need to add the above lines to the script or service definition to ensure the right environment is loaded.
- **Check which node is being used:**
  ```bash
  which node
  which npm
  which npx
  ```
  They should all point to your `~/.nvm/versions/node/v22.9.0/bin/` directory.

---

### 6. **Summary Table**

| Tool | What it does | How it’s managed |
|------|--------------|------------------|
| nvm  | Manages Node.js versions | Shell function, not a binary |
| npm  | Installs packages | Comes with Node.js |
| npx  | Runs binaries from npm packages | Comes with Node.js |

---

### 7. **Why the Error?**

- If the MCP tool is run in an environment where `nvm` is not activated, it might use a system Node.js (which may not have `fetch`), or it might not find the right packages.
- If you want to guarantee the right Node.js is used, always activate `nvm` in the shell or script before running the command.

---

**Would you like a script or service template that ensures `nvm` is activated before running your MCP tool? Or do you want to debug your current environment further?**

Great question! Here’s what’s happening and why you’re seeing this mismatch:

---

### 1. **Your Node.js vs npm/npx Paths**

- **Node.js (`node`)**
  - Path: `/usr/bin/node`
  - Version: `v20.19.1`

- **npm & npx**
  - Path: `/usr/bin/npm` and `/usr/bin/npx`
  - Version: `10.8.2`

---

### 2. **Why the Discrepancy?**

- **System Node.js Installation:**  
  Your `node`, `npm`, and `npx` are all coming from `/usr/bin/`, which means they are the system-installed versions (likely installed via your package manager, e.g., `apt`).

- **nvm Node.js Installation:**  
  When you use `nvm`, it installs Node.js, npm, and npx in your home directory (e.g., `~/.nvm/versions/node/vXX.X.X/bin/`).  
  But in your current shell, you are not using the `nvm` versions—your `PATH` is prioritizing `/usr/bin/`.

- **Version Numbers:**  
  - Node.js v20.19.1 is a recent version, but it’s the system version, not the one managed by `nvm`.
  - npm and npx versions are tied to the Node.js version, but again, you’re using the system ones.

---

### 3. **How This Happens**

- If you open a new terminal and don’t run `nvm use <version>`, your shell defaults to the system Node.js, npm, and npx.
- If you install Node.js via `apt` or another system package manager, it puts binaries in `/usr/bin/`.
- If you want to use the `nvm`-managed versions, you must activate them in your shell.

---

### 4. **How to Fix**

**Activate nvm in your shell:**
```bash
export NVM_DIR="$HOME/.nvm"
[ -s "$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh" ] && \. "$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh"
nvm use 22.9.0  # or whatever version you want
```

**Then check:**
```bash
which node
which npm
which npx
node -v
npm -v
npx -v
```
They should all point to `~/.nvm/versions/node/vXX.X.X/bin/` and show the versions managed by `nvm`.

---

### 5. **Summary Table**

| Tool   | System Path      | nvm Path (when active)                        |
|--------|------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| node   | /usr/bin/node    | ~/.nvm/versions/node/v22.9.0/bin/node         |
| npm    | /usr/bin/npm     | ~/.nvm/versions/node/v22.9.0/bin/npm          |
| npx    | /usr/bin/npx     | ~/.nvm/versions/node/v22.9.0/bin/npx          |

---

### 6. **Why This Matters**

- If you want to use packages or features (like global `fetch`) from a newer Node.js, you must ensure all tools (`node`, `npm`, `npx`) are from the same environment.
- If your MCP tool or scripts are run in a context that doesn’t activate `nvm`, they’ll use the system versions, which may not have the features or packages you expect.

This tool helps analyze problems through a flexible thinking process that can adapt and evolve.

When to use this tool:
- Breaking down complex problems into steps
- Planning and design with room for revision
- Analysis that might need course correction
- Problems where the full scope might not be clear initially
- Problems that require a multi-step solution
- Tasks that need to maintain context over multiple steps
- Situations where irrelevant information needs to be filtered out

Key features:
- You can adjust total_thoughts up or down as you progress
- You can question or revise previous thoughts
- You can add more thoughts even after reaching what seemed like the end
- You can express uncertainty and explore alternative approaches
- Not every thought needs to build linearly - you can branch or backtrack
- Generates a solution hypothesis
- Verifies the hypothesis based on the Chain of Thought steps
- Repeats the process until satisfied
- Provides a correct answer

Parameters explained:
- thought: Your current thinking step, which can include:
* Regular analytical steps
* Revisions of previous thoughts
* Questions about previous decisions
* Realizations about needing more analysis
* Changes in approach
* Hypothesis generation
* Hypothesis verification
- next_thought_needed: True if you need more thinking, even if at what seemed like the end
- thought_number: Current number in sequence (can go beyond initial total if needed)
- total_thoughts: Current estimate of thoughts needed (can be adjusted up/down)
- is_revision: A boolean indicating if this thought revises previous thinking
- revises_thought: If is_revision is true, which thought number is being reconsidered
- branch_from_thought: If branching, which thought number is the branching point
- branch_id: Identifier for the current branch (if any)
- needs_more_thoughts: If reaching end but realizing more thoughts needed

You should:
1. Start with an initial estimate of needed thoughts, but be ready to adjust
2. Feel free to question or revise previous thoughts
3. Don't hesitate to add more thoughts if needed, even at the "end"
4. Express uncertainty when present
5. Mark thoughts that revise previous thinking or branch into new paths
6. Ignore information that is irrelevant to the current step
7. Generate a solution hypothesis when appropriate
8. Verify the hypothesis based on the Chain of Thought steps
9. Repeat the process until satisfied with the solution
10. Provide a single, ideally correct answer as the final output
11. Only set next_thought_needed to false when truly done and a satisfactory answer is reached

To fix, ensure nvm is sourced in all relevant shell init files and that PATH is set correctly before running node/npm/npx commands.

---

Here’s a step-by-step reasoning for why your nvm path is being ignored:

1. If nvm is not sourced in your shell initialization files (like `.bashrc`, `.zshrc`, or `.profile`), your shell won’t know about nvm, and the system will default to using `/usr/bin/node`, `/usr/bin/npm`, and `/usr/bin/npx`.
2. If you run commands from a script, GUI, or background process, those may launch a non-interactive shell that does not source your user profile, so nvm never gets activated.
3. If your PATH environment variable lists `/usr/bin` before the nvm directory, the system binaries will be used even if nvm is sourced later or incompletely.
4. In summary, the nvm path is ignored because either nvm isn’t sourced, PATH isn’t updated, or the process context doesn’t load your shell configs. 

To fix this, ensure nvm is sourced in all relevant shell init files and that your PATH is set to prioritize nvm’s binaries before running node, npm, or npx commands. If you need, I can provide a shell snippet or troubleshooting steps to guarantee nvm is always used.

_**User (2025-06-02 03:01Z)**_

ok then how do i update by system node and npx ?

---

_**Assistant**_

To update your **system** Node.js, npm, and npx (the ones in `/usr/bin/`), you have a few options. Here’s a clear guide for Ubuntu/Linux systems:

---

## 1. **Update via NodeSource (Recommended for Most Users)**

NodeSource provides up-to-date Node.js binaries for most Linux distributions.

**a. Remove old Node.js (optional but recommended):**
```bash
sudo apt remove nodejs npm
```

**b. Add the NodeSource repository for your desired version (e.g., Node.js 22):**
```bash
curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_22.x | sudo -E bash -
```

**c. Install Node.js (includes npm and npx):**
```bash
sudo apt install -y nodejs
```

**d. Verify:**
```bash
node -v
npm -v
npx -v
which node
which npm
which npx
```
They should all point to `/usr/bin/` and show the new version.

---

## 2. **Update via Ubuntu’s Package Manager (Not Always Latest)**

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install nodejs npm
```
> Note: This may not give you the latest version. NodeSource is preferred for up-to-date releases.

---

## 3. **If You Want to Use nvm Instead (Recommended for Devs)**

- nvm is best for managing multiple Node.js versions per user, but it does not update `/usr/bin/node`.
- If you want system-wide updates, use NodeSource as above.

---

## 4. **Why Update System Node.js?**

- System scripts, services, or apps that don’t use nvm will use `/usr/bin/node`.
- Keeping it updated ensures compatibility and security.

---

## 5. **Caution**

- If you use both nvm and system Node.js, be aware which one is active in your shell (`which node`).
- System updates may overwrite your Node.js version.

---
